title: A 40-year plan for energy - Amory Lovins

analysis.summary: La vidéo présente une conférence d'Amory Lovins, fondateur du Rocky Mountain Institute, intitulée « A 40-year plan for energy ». L'intervention commence par une interrogation rhétorique puissante : face aux choix entre les guerres du pétrole, le changement climatique, l'holocauste nucléaire ou la combinaison de tous ces maux, l'orateur propose une cinquième option, l'option E : « aucun de ceux-là ». Il introduit le concept de « réinventer le feu », métaphore pour passer des combustibles fossiles à un système énergétique moderne, sûr et durable. Lovins souligne que quatre cinquièmes de l'énergie mondiale proviennent encore de la combustion de ressources fossiles, ce qui a bâti la civilisation mais engendre désormais des coûts cachés dépassant les bénéfices en termes de sécurité, d'économie, de santé et d'environnement. Il identifie deux récits principaux à changer : celui du pétrole et celui de l'électricité, chacun représentant deux cinquièmes des émissions de carbone fossile. Le plan proposé vise à éliminer la dépendance au pétrole et au charbon d'ici 2050, tout en réduisant l'usage du gaz naturel d'un tiers. Cette transition, selon les calculs de Lovins, coûterait 5 000 milliards de dollars de moins en valeur actualisée nette que le scénario « business as usual », tout en permettant à l'économie américaine de croître de 158 %. Une caractéristique centrale de cette proposition est qu'elle ne nécessite aucune nouvelle invention, aucune loi fédérale, aucun subside ni aucune taxe, mais repose sur l'innovation privée et l'efficacité énergétique. Lovins détaille ensuite la transformation du secteur des transports. Il explique que les deux tiers de l'énergie d'une voiture sont gaspillés à déplacer son propre poids. En utilisant des matériaux ultra-légers comme les composites de fibre de carbone, on peut réduire drastiquement la masse des véhicules, ce qui permet de réduire la taille des moteurs et des batteries, rendant la propulsion électrique abordable. Il cite des exemples concrets comme les annonces de Volkswagen et BMW concernant des véhicules à fibre de carbone, et décrit une technologie de fabrication américaine rapide et peu coûteuse pour ces matériaux. Cette « fitness » des véhicules, combinée à des stratégies de prix incitatifs (frais sur les véhicules inefficaces, subventions pour les efficaces), pourrait doubler les économies de pétrole et rendre l'électrification dominante. Lovins mentionne également l'efficacité dans le transport lourd (camions Walmart) et aérien, ainsi que la réduction du besoin de se déplacer grâce à une meilleure planification urbaine et aux technologies de l'information, ce qui pourrait réduire les déplacements de 46 à 84 %. Il introduit le concept d'« acupuncture institutionnelle » pour débloquer les goulots d'étranglement économiques. Ensuite, il aborde le secteur de l'électricité. L'efficacité énergétique dans les bâtiments et l'industrie est présentée comme la ressource la moins chère. Il cite la rénovation de l'Empire State Building comme exemple de « design intégratif » qui a permis de réduire la consommation de plus de 40 % en trois ans grâce à une approche systémique plutôt que technologique isolée. Dans l'industrie, il explique comment la simple réorganisation des tuyauteries peut réduire la consommation d'énergie des pompes de 86 %, illustrant le principe des pertes cumulées en amont. Lovins argue que la réduction de la demande facilite le passage aux énergies renouvelables. Il note que les coûts du solaire ont chuté de manière spectaculaire et que les investissements privés dans les renouvelables dépassent désormais ceux du nucléaire et du charbon. Il conteste l'idée que les énergies renouvelables sont intermittentes et peu fiables, en soulignant que tous les générateurs tombent en panne et que la diversité géographique et technologique, couplée à une gestion intelligente, assure la fiabilité. Il propose un réseau électrique décentralisé et résilient, capable de fonctionner en micro-réseaux, inspiré des pratiques militaires pour la sécurité nationale. Enfin, Lovins conclut en affirmant que cette transition offre une opportunité commerciale majeure, renforçant la sécurité nationale, la santé publique et l'environnement, tout en étant économiquement avantageuse. Il invite l'audience à rejoindre ce mouvement via le site reinventingfire.com, présentant ce changement comme une opportunité historique de créer un monde plus riche, juste, frais et sûr.
key_points: L'orateur propose une cinquième option (« aucun de ceux-là ») face aux dangers du pétrole, du climat et du nucléaire.
Quatre cinquièmes de l'énergie mondiale proviennent encore de la combustion de ressources fossiles.
Les coûts cachés des combustibles fossiles dépassent désormais leurs bénéfices économiques et sociaux.
Le plan vise à éliminer la dépendance au pétrole et au charbon d'ici 2050.
La transition énergétique coûterait 5 000 milliards de dollars de moins que le scénario actuel.
L'économie américaine pourrait croître de 158 % grâce à cette transition énergétique.
La stratégie ne nécessite aucune nouvelle invention, loi fédérale ou subvention gouvernementale.
Deux tiers de l'énergie d'une voiture sont gaspillés à déplacer son propre poids.
Les matériaux ultra-légers comme la fibre de carbone permettent de réduire drastiquement la masse des véhicules.
La réduction du poids des véhicules rend la propulsion électrique économiquement viable.
Les technologies de fabrication de la fibre de carbone peuvent être adaptées à la production automobile de masse.
Les programmes de frais et subventions incitatifs ont triplé la vitesse d'amélioration de l'efficacité automobile en Europe.
L'électrification des véhicules transforme l'industrie automobile de manière disruptive, similaire au passage à l'informatique.
L'efficacité logistique et la conception peuvent réduire la consommation de carburant des camions de 60 %.
L'innovation militaire en efficacité énergétique accélère les avancées civiles.
L'acupuncturation institutionnelle permet de débloquer les goulots d'étranglement économiques dans les entreprises.
L'efficacité énergétique dans les bâtiments peut tripler ou quadrupler sa productivité énergétique.
Le design intégratif permet d'obtenir des économies d'énergie plus importantes à moindre coût.
La rénovation de l'Empire State Building a réduit sa consommation d'énergie de plus de 40 %.
La réorganisation des systèmes de tuyauterie industrielle peut réduire la consommation d'énergie des pompes de 86 %.
Les pertes cumulées dans la chaîne énergétique signifient qu'une unité d'économie en bout de chaîne économise dix unités en amont.
Les coûts des modules solaires ont chuté de manière spectaculaire, dépassant les prévisions.
Les investissements privés dans les énergies renouvelables ont dépassé ceux du nucléaire et du charbon.
Tous les générateurs électriques tombent en panne, rendant la diversité et la redondance essentielles.
Les réseaux électriques décentralisés et les micro-réseaux offrent une meilleure résilience face aux catastrophes.
Le modèle de réseau décentralisé est inspiré des stratégies de sécurité énergétique du Pentagone.
La réduction de la demande d'électricité facilite l'intégration des sources renouvelables variables.
Les véhicules électriques intelligents peuvent fournir des services de stockage et de flexibilité au réseau électrique.
L'efficacité énergétique dans l'industrie représente une opportunité d'économie de 500 milliards de dollars.
Le design intégratif dans l'industrie peut doubler la productivité énergétique avec un retour sur investissement rapide.
Les renouvelables autres que l'hydroélectricité massive ont reçu 151 milliards de dollars d'investissement privé en 2010.
La capacité de production mondiale de cellules solaires augmente de 60 à 70 % par an.
Aucune nouvelle centrale nucléaire n'a pu lever de capitaux privés malgré des subventions massives.
L'efficacité et le gaz naturel peuvent remplacer les centrales au charbon à un coût inférieur à leur coût de fonctionnement.
Les réseaux renouvelables diversifiés géographiquement peuvent fournir une électricité fiable et prévisible.
L'Allemagne, le Portugal et le Danemark ont atteint des parts significatives d'énergie éolienne et renouvelable.
Le coût de remplacement du réseau électrique existant est similaire quel que soit le mix énergétique choisi.
Les micro-réseaux peuvent se déconnecter et se reconnecter de manière transparente pour assurer la continuité.
L'intensité énergétique du PIB américain a été divisée par deux depuis 1976 grâce à l'efficacité.
La transition réduit les émissions de carbone fossile de 82 à 86 %.
L'orateur invite l'audience à s'engager via le site reinventingfire.com pour capturer cette opportunité économique.
analysis.analysis:
### 1. Forces du contenu
L'argumentation d'Amory Lovins se distingue par sa rigueur économique et sa vision systémique. Il ne présente pas l'efficacité énergétique comme une contrainte environnementale, mais comme une opportunité commerciale majeure, ce qui rend son discours persuasif pour un public d'hommes d'affaires et de décideurs. L'utilisation d'exemples concrets, tels que la rénovation de l'Empire State Building ou les stratégies de Walmart, ancre ses théories dans la réalité opérationnelle. La structure logique, passant du problème global aux solutions sectorielles (transports, électricité) puis à la synthèse, facilite la compréhension d'un sujet complexe. De plus, l'approche « sans nouvelle invention » désamorce les scepticismes technologiques et rend le plan immédiatement réalisable.

### 2. Concepts centraux
Le concept central est le « design intégratif », qui consiste à optimiser l'ensemble d'un système plutôt que d'améliorer des composants isolés, permettant ainsi des économies d'échelle et de synergies. Lovins introduit également l'« acupuncturation institutionnelle », une méthode pour identifier et corriger les distorsions de marché qui empêchent l'adoption de technologies efficientes. La notion de « fitness » des véhicules, où la réduction du poids et de la traînée est prioritaire sur la simple amélioration du moteur, est un autre pilier. Enfin, l'idée que la demande d'énergie peut être réduite plus rapidement que la croissance économique, grâce à l'efficacité, remet en cause le dogme de la corrélation positive entre consommation énergétique et PIB.

### 3. Implications et perspectives
Les implications de ce plan sont profondes : une indépendance énergétique totale des États-Unis, une réduction drastique de la pollution et une amélioration de la sécurité nationale grâce à un réseau électrique décentralisé. Lovins suggère que cette transition pourrait redéfinir la compétitivité industrielle, avec les États-Unis menant une nouvelle révolution automobile et énergétique. À long terme, cela implique une transformation structurelle des modèles d'affaires, passant de la vente de volume (kWh, gallons) à la vente de services et d'efficacité. Cela ouvre également la voie à une géopolitique de l'énergie moins conflictuelle, basée sur des ressources locales et renouvelables plutôt que sur des hydrocarbures concentrés.

### 4. Faiblesses et limites
Une limite majeure est l'hypothèse selon laquelle la transition peut se faire sans intervention gouvernementale significative (pas de nouvelles lois, taxes ou subventions). Bien que Lovins mentionne des mécanismes de marché, le déploiement à l'échelle nécessaire pourrait nécessiter des cadres réglementaires plus forts que ceux existants. De plus, l'estimation d'une croissance économique de 158 % semble optimiste et dépend fortement de la capacité de l'économie à absorber les gains d'efficacité sans récession structurelle. La dépendance résiduelle au gaz naturel et aux biocarburants avancés n'est pas entièrement détaillée en termes de disponibilité et de durabilité à long terme.

### 5. Contradictions internes
Il n'y a pas de contradictions logiques flagrantes dans le propos, mais il existe une tension entre l'accent mis sur l'innovation privée et la réalité des barrières réglementaires actuelles qui favorisent les énergies établies. Lovins affirme que le marché est le meilleur vecteur, tout en admettant que les institutions actuelles sont inefficaces et doivent être contournées. Cette approche « par-dessus » les institutions pourrait être vue comme contradictoire avec l'idée d'une transition fluide et démocratique, bien qu'elle soit présentée comme une nécessité pragmatique.

### 6. Biais cognitifs et rhétoriques
Lovins utilise un biais d'optimisme technologique et économique, en supposant que les tendances passées de baisse des coûts (solaire, fibre de carbone) se poursuivront indéfiniment. Il emploie également un appel à l'autorité en citant des figures militaires et économiques prestigieuses (Eisenhower, Friedman). Le ton est parfois péremptoire, présentant sa solution comme la seule logique et économiquement inévitable, ce qui peut minimiser les défis politiques et sociaux de la transition. L'expression « big discoveries are protected by public incredulity » est une rhétorique de défense contre le scepticisme, renforçant le biais de confirmation chez les partisans.

### 7. Limites de cette analyse
Cette analyse se base uniquement sur la transcription fournie, qui représente une présentation orale et non un document technique complet. Les détails chiffrés, bien que précis, ne sont pas sourcés dans le texte et nécessiteraient une vérification externe. L'analyse ne peut pas évaluer la faisabilité politique réelle du plan, car la transcription omet les débats parlementaires ou les résistances syndicales potentielles. De plus, le contexte international est limité aux États-Unis, bien que des références soient faites à l'Allemagne et à la Chine.

### 8. Contexte manquant
Le contexte manquant inclut les détails sur la chaîne d'approvisionnement de la fibre de carbone à grande échelle et les impacts environnementaux de sa production. La transcription ne discute pas des défis liés au recyclage des batteries et des panneaux solaires en fin de vie. Il manque également une analyse des coûts sociaux de la transition, comme la perte d'emplois dans les secteurs fossiles, qui n'est pas abordée en profondeur. Enfin, le rôle précis des micro-réseaux dans les zones rurales ou pauvres, où l'infrastructure est faible, n'est pas développé.

metadata.title: A 40-year plan for energy - Amory Lovins

transcription.text: America's public energy conversation boils down to this question. Would you rather die of A, oil wars, or B, climate change, or C, nuclear holocaust, or D, all of the above? Oh, I missed one. Or E, none of the above. That's the one we're not normally offered. What if we could make energy do our work without working our undoing? Could we have fuel without fear? Could we reinvent fire? You see, fire made us human. Fossil fuels made us modern. But now we need a new fire that makes us safe, secure, healthy, and durable. Let's see how. Four fifths of the world's energy still comes from burning each year four cubic miles of the rotted remains of primeval swamp goo. Those fossil fuels have built our civilization. They've created our wealth. They've enriched the lives of billions. But they also have rising costs to our security, economy, health, and environment that are starting to erode, if not outweigh their benefits. So we need a new fire. And switching from the old fire to the new fire means changing two big stories about oil and electricity. Each of which puts two fifths of the fossil carbon in the air, but they're really quite distinct. Less than 1% of our electricity is made from oil, although almost half is made from coal. Their uses are quite concentrated. Three fourths of our oil fuels transportation. Three fourths of our electricity powers buildings, and the rest of both runs factories. So very efficient vehicles, buildings, and factories save oil and coal, and also natural gas that can displace both of them. But today's energy system is not just inefficient. It is also disconnected, aging, dirty, and insecure. So it needs refurbishment. By 2050 though, it could become efficient, connected, and distributed with elegantly frugal autos, factories, and buildings, all relying on a modern, secure, and resilient electricity system. We can eliminate our addiction to oil and coal by 2050 and use one third less natural gas while switching to efficient use and renewable supply. This could cost by 2050 $5 trillion less in net present value that is expressed as a lump sum today than business as usual, assuming that carbon emissions and all other hidden or external costs are worth zero, a conservatively low estimate. Yet this cheaper energy system could support a 158% bigger US economy, all without needing oil or coal, or for that matter, nuclear energy. Moreover, this transition needs no new inventions, and no acts of Congress, and no new federal taxes, mandates, subsidies, or laws, and running Washington gridlock. Let me say that again. I'm going to tell you how to get the United States completely off oil and coal $5 trillion cheaper with no act of Congress led by business for profit. In other words, we're going to use our most effective institutions, private enterprise, co-evolving with civil society, and sped by military innovation to go around our least effective institutions. And whether you care most about profits and jobs and competitive advantage, or national security, or environmental stewardship, and climate protection, and public health, reinventing fire makes sense and makes money. General Eisenhower reputedly said that enlarging the boundaries of a tough problem makes it soluble by encompassing more options and more synergies. So in reinventing fire, we integrated all four sectors that use energy, transportation, buildings, industry, and electricity. And we integrated four kinds of innovation, not just technology and policy, but also design and business strategy. Those combinations yield very much more than the sum of the parts, especially in creating deeply disruptive business opportunities. Oil costs our economy $2 billion a day, plus another $4 billion a day in hidden economic and military costs, raising its total cost to over a sixth of GDP. Our mobility fuel goes three-fifths to automobiles. So let's start by making autos oil free. Two-thirds of the energy it takes to move a typical car is caused by its weight. And every unit of energy you save at the wheels, by taking out weight or drag, saves seven units at the tank, cuz you don't have to weigh six units getting the energy to the wheels. Unfortunately, over the past quarter century, epidemic obesity has made our two-ton steel cars gain weight twice as fast as we have, but today, ultra-light, ultra-strong materials, like carbon fiber composites, can make dramatic weight-saving snowball and can make cars simpler and cheaper to build. Lighter and more slippery autos need less force to move them, so their engines get smaller. Indeed, that sort of vehicle fitness then makes electric propulsion affordable, because the batteries or fuel cells also get smaller and lighter and cheaper. So sticker prices will ultimately fall to about the same as today, while the driving cost even from the start is very much lower. So these innovations together can transform automakers from wringing tiny savings out of Victorian engine and steel stamping technologies to the steeply falling costs of three linked innovations that strongly reinforce each other, namely ultra-light materials, making them into structures and electric propulsion. The sales can grow and the prices fall even faster with temporary fee baits, that is rebates for efficient new autos paid for by fees on inefficient ones. And just in the first two years, the biggest of Europe's five fee bait programs has tripled the speed of improving automotive efficiency. The resulting shift to electric autos is going to be as game-changing as shifting from typewriters to the gains in computers. Of course, computers and electronics are now America's biggest industry, while typewriter makers have vanished. So vehicle fitness opens a new automotive competitive strategy that can double the oil savings over the next 40 years, but then also make electrification affordable, and that displaces the rest of the oil. America could lead this next automotive revolution. Currently, the leader is Germany. Last year, Volkswagen announced that by next year, they'll be producing this carbon fiber plug-in hybrid getting 230 miles a gallon. Also last year, BMW announced this carbon fiber electric car. They said that its carbon fiber is paid for by needing fewer batteries. And they said, we do not intend to be a typewriter maker. Audi claimed it's going to beat them both by a year. Seven years ago, an even faster and cheaper American manufacturing technology was used to make this little carbon fiber test part, which doubles as a carbon cap. In one minute, and you can tell from the sound how immensely stiff and strong it is. Don't worry about dropping it. It's tougher than titanium. Tom Friedman actually whacked it as hard as he could with a sledgehammer without even scuffing it. But such manufacturing techniques can scale to automotive speed and cost with aerospace performance. They can save four-fifths of the capital needed to make autos. They can save lives because this stuff can absorb up to 12 times as much crash energy per pound of steel. If we made all our autos this way, it would save oil equivalent to finding one and a half Saudi Arabias or half an OPEC by drilling in the Detroit formation, a very perspective play. All those nega barrels under Detroit cost an average of 18 bucks a barrel. They are all-American, carbon-free, and inexhaustible. The same physics and the same business logic also apply to big vehicles. In the five years ending in 2010, Walmart saved 60% of the fuel per ton mile in its giant fleet of heavy trucks through better logistics and design, but just the technological savings in heavy trucks can get to thirds and combined with triple to quintupled efficiency airplanes now on the drawing board can save close to a trillion dollars. Also today's military revolution in energy efficiency is going to speed up all of these civilian advances in much the same way that military R&D has given us the internet, the global positioning system, and the jet engine and microchip industries. As we design and build vehicles better, we can also use them smarter by harnessing four powerful techniques for eliminating needless driving. Instead of just seeing the travel grow, we can use innovative pricing, charging for road infrastructure by the mile, not by the gallon. We can use some smart IT to enhance transit and enable car sharing and ride sharing. We can allow smart and lucrative growth models that help people already be near where they want to be so they don't need to go somewhere else and we can use smart IT to make traffic free flowing. Together those things can give us the same or better access with 46 to 84 percent less driving, saving another point four trillion dollars plus point three trillion dollars from using trucks more productively. So 40 years hence, when you add it all up, a far more mobile U.S. economy can use no oil. Saving or displacing barrels for 25 bucks rather than buying them for over a hundred adds up to a four trillion dollar net saving, counting all the hidden costs at zero. So to get mobility without oil, to phase out the oil, we can get efficient and then switch fuels. Those 125 to 240 mile per gallon equivalent autos can use any mixture of hydrogen fuel cells, electricity, and advanced biofuels. The trucks and planes can realistically use hydrogen or advanced biofuels. The trucks could even use natural gas, but no vehicles will need oil. And the most biofuel we might need, just three million barrels a day, can be made two-thirds from waste without displacing any cropland and without harming soil or climate. Our team speeds up these kinds of oil savings by what we call institutional acupuncture. We figure out where the business logic is congested and not flowing properly. We stick little needles in it to get it flowing, working with partners like Ford and Walmart and the Pentagon. And the long transition is already well underway. In fact, three years ago, mainstream analysts were starting to see peak oil not in supply, but in demand. And Deutsche Bank even said world oil use could peak around 2016. In other words, oil is getting uncompetitive even at low prices before it becomes unavailable even at high prices. But the electrified vehicles don't need to burden the electricity grid. Rather, when smart autos exchange electricity and information through smart buildings with smart grids, they're adding to the grid valuable flexibility and storage that help the grid integrate varying solar and wind power. So the electrified autos make the auto and electricity problems easier to solve together than separately. And they also converge the oil story with our second big story, saving electricity and then making it differently. And those twin revolutions in electricity will bring to that sector more numerous and profound and diverse disruptions than any other sector because we've got 21st century technology and speed colliding head on with 20th and 19th century institutions, rules, and cultures. Changing how we make electricity gets easier if we need less of it. Most of it now is wasted and the technologies for saving it keep improving faster than we're installing them. So the unbought efficiency resource keeps getting ever bigger and cheaper. But as efficiency in buildings and industry starts to grow faster than the economy, America's electricity use could actually shrink even with the little extra use required for those efficient electrified autos. And we can do this just by reasonably accelerating existing trends. Over the next 40 years, buildings which use three-quarters of the electricity can triple or quadruple their energy productivity, saving $1.4 trillion dollars net present value with a 33% internal rate of return or in English the savings are worth four times what they cost. And industry can accelerate too doubling its energy productivity with a 21% internal rate of return. The key is a disruptive innovation that we call integrative design that often makes very big energy savings cost less than small or no savings. That is it can give you expanding returns, not diminishing returns. That is how our 2010 retrofit is saving over two-fifths of the energy in the Empire State Building. Remanufacturing those six and a half thousand windows on site into super windows that pass light but reflect heat plus better lights and office equipment and such cut the maximum cooling load by a third and then renovating smaller chillers instead of adding bigger ones saved 17 million dollars of capital cost which helped pay for the other improvements and reduce the payback to just three years. Integrative design can also increase energy savings in industry. Dow's billion-dollar efficiency investment has already returned nine billion dollars but industry as a whole has another half trillion dollars of energy still to save. For example, three-fifths of the world's electricity runs motors, half of that runs pumps and fans and those can all be made more efficient and the motors that turn them can have their system efficiency roughly doubled by integrating 35 improvements paying back in about a year but first we ought to be capturing bigger cheaper savings that are normally ignored and are not in the textbooks. For example, pumps, the biggest use of motors move liquid through pipes but a standard industrial pumping loop was redesigned to use at least 86 percent less energy not by getting better pumps but just by replacing long thin crooked pipes with fat short straight pipes. This is not about new technology it's just rearranging our metal furniture and of course it also shrinks the pumping equipment and its capital costs. So what does such savings mean for the electricity that is three-fifths used in motors? Well from the coal burned at the power plant through all these compounding losses only a tenth of the fuel energy actually ends up coming out the pipe as flow but now let's turn those compounding losses around backwards and every unit of flow or friction that we save in the pipe saves 10 units of fuel cost pollution and what Hunter Levenson calls global weirding back at the power plant and of course as you go back upstream the components get smaller and therefore cheaper. Our team has lately found such snowballing energy savings in more than 30 billion dollars worth of industrial redesigns everything from data centers and ship fabs to mines and refineries. Typically our retrofit designs save about 30 to 60 percent of the energy and pay back in a few years while the new facility design save 40 to 90 odd percent with generally lower capital cost. Now needing less electricity would ease and speed the shift to new sources of electricity chiefly renewables. China leads their explosive growth and their plummeting cost in fact these solar power module costs have just fallen off the bottom of the chart and Germany now has more solar workers than America has steel workers. Already in about 20 states private installers will come put those cheap solar cells on your roof with no money down and beat your utility bill. Such unregulated products could ultimately add up to a virtual utility that bypasses your electric company just as your cell phone bypassed your wireline phone company and this sort of thing gives utility executives the heebie-jeebies and it gives venture capitalists sweet dreams. Renewables are no longer a fringe activity for each of the past four years half of the world's new generating capacity has been renewable mainly lately in developing countries. In 2010 renewables other than big hydro particularly wind and solar cells got 151 billion dollars of private investment and they actually surpassed the total installed capacity of nuclear power in the world by adding 60 billion watts in that one year. That happens to be the same amount of solar cell capacity that the world can now make every year a number that goes up 60 or 70 percent a year. In contrast the net additions of nuclear capacity and coal capacity and the orders behind those keep fading because they cost too much and they have too much financial risk. In fact in this country no new nuclear power plant has been able to raise any private construction capital despite seven years of 100 plus percent subsidies. So how else could we replace the coal fired power plants? Well efficiency and gas can displace them all at below just their operating cost and combined with renewables can displace them more than 23 times at less than their replacement cost but we only need to replace them once. We're often told though that only coal and nuclear plants can keep the lights on because they're 24 seven whereas wind and solar power are variable and hence supposedly unreliable. Actually no generator is 24 seven they all break and when a big plant goes down you lose a thousand megawatts in milliseconds often for weeks or months often without warning. That is exactly why we've designed the grid to back up failed plants with working plants and in exactly the same way the grid can handle wind wind and solar power's forecastable variations. Hourly simulations show that largely or wholly renewable grids can deliver highly reliable power when they're forecasted integrated and diversified by both type and location and that's true both for continental areas like the U.S. or Europe and for smaller areas embedded within a larger grid. That is how for example for German states in 2010 were 43 to 52 percent wind-powered Portugal was 45 percent renewable-powered Denmark 36 and it's how all of Europe can shift to renewable electricity. In America our aging dirty and insecure power system has to be replaced anyway by 2050 and whatever we replace it with is going to cost about the same about six trillion dollars net present value segments.text: America's public energy conversation boils down to this question.
Would you rather die of A, oil wars, or B, climate change, or C, nuclear holocaust, or
D, all of the above?
Oh, I missed one.
Or E, none of the above.
That's the one we're not normally offered.
What if we could make energy do our work without working our undoing?
Could we have fuel without fear?
Could we reinvent fire?
You see, fire made us human.
Fossil fuels made us modern.
But now we need a new fire that makes us safe, secure, healthy, and durable.
Let's see how.
Four fifths of the world's energy still comes from burning each year four cubic
miles of the rotted remains of primeval swamp goo.
Those fossil fuels have built our civilization.
They've created our wealth.
They've enriched the lives of billions.
But they also have rising costs to our security, economy, health, and
environment that are starting to erode, if not outweigh their benefits.
So we need a new fire.
And switching from the old fire to the new fire means changing two big
stories about oil and electricity.
Each of which puts two fifths of the fossil carbon in the air, but
they're really quite distinct.
Less than 1% of our electricity is made from oil,
although almost half is made from coal.
Their uses are quite concentrated.
Three fourths of our oil fuels transportation.
Three fourths of our electricity powers buildings, and
the rest of both runs factories.
So very efficient vehicles, buildings, and
factories save oil and coal, and also natural gas that can
displace both of them.
But today's energy system is not just inefficient.
It is also disconnected, aging, dirty, and insecure.
So it needs refurbishment.
By 2050 though, it could become efficient, connected, and
distributed with elegantly frugal autos, factories, and buildings,
all relying on a modern, secure, and resilient electricity system.
We can eliminate our addiction to oil and coal by 2050 and
use one third less natural gas while switching to efficient use and
renewable supply.
This could cost by 2050 $5 trillion less in net present value
that is expressed as a lump sum today than business as usual,
assuming that carbon emissions and all other hidden or
external costs are worth zero, a conservatively low estimate.
Yet this cheaper energy system could support a 158% bigger US economy,
all without needing oil or coal, or for that matter, nuclear energy.
Moreover, this transition needs no new inventions, and no acts of Congress,
and no new federal taxes, mandates, subsidies, or
laws, and running Washington gridlock.
Let me say that again.
I'm going to tell you how to get the United States completely off oil and
coal $5 trillion cheaper with no act of Congress led by business for profit.
In other words, we're going to use our most effective institutions,
private enterprise, co-evolving with civil society, and
sped by military innovation to go around our least effective institutions.
And whether you care most about profits and jobs and competitive advantage, or
national security, or environmental stewardship, and climate protection, and
public health, reinventing fire makes sense and makes money.
General Eisenhower reputedly said that enlarging the boundaries of a tough problem
makes it soluble by encompassing more options and more synergies.
So in reinventing fire, we integrated all four sectors that use energy,
transportation, buildings, industry, and electricity.
And we integrated four kinds of innovation, not just technology and
policy, but also design and business strategy.
Those combinations yield very much more than the sum of the parts,
especially in creating deeply disruptive business opportunities.
Oil costs our economy $2 billion a day, plus another $4 billion a day in
hidden economic and military costs, raising its total cost to over a sixth
of GDP.
Our mobility fuel goes three-fifths to automobiles.
So let's start by making autos oil free.
Two-thirds of the energy it takes to move a typical car is caused by its weight.
And every unit of energy you save at the wheels, by taking out weight or
drag, saves seven units at the tank,
cuz you don't have to weigh six units getting the energy to the wheels.
Unfortunately, over the past quarter century,
epidemic obesity has made our two-ton steel cars gain weight twice as fast
as we have, but today, ultra-light, ultra-strong materials,
like carbon fiber composites, can make dramatic weight-saving snowball and
can make cars simpler and cheaper to build.
Lighter and more slippery autos need less force to move them,
so their engines get smaller.
Indeed, that sort of vehicle fitness then makes electric
propulsion affordable, because the batteries or
fuel cells also get smaller and lighter and cheaper.
So sticker prices will ultimately fall to about the same as today,
while the driving cost even from the start is very much lower.
So these innovations together can transform automakers from
wringing tiny savings out of Victorian engine and
steel stamping technologies to the steeply falling costs of three
linked innovations that strongly reinforce each other,
namely ultra-light materials, making them into structures and
electric propulsion.
The sales can grow and the prices fall even faster with temporary fee baits,
that is rebates for efficient new autos paid for by fees on inefficient ones.
And just in the first two years, the biggest of Europe's five fee bait
programs has tripled the speed of improving automotive efficiency.
The resulting shift to electric autos is going to be as game-changing as
shifting from typewriters to the gains in computers.
Of course, computers and
electronics are now America's biggest industry, while typewriter makers
have vanished.
So vehicle fitness opens a new automotive competitive strategy
that can double the oil savings over the next 40 years, but
then also make electrification affordable, and
that displaces the rest of the oil.
America could lead this next automotive revolution.
Currently, the leader is Germany.
Last year, Volkswagen announced that by next year,
they'll be producing this carbon fiber plug-in hybrid getting 230 miles a gallon.
Also last year, BMW announced this carbon fiber electric car.
They said that its carbon fiber is paid for by needing fewer batteries.
And they said, we do not intend to be a typewriter maker.
Audi claimed it's going to beat them both by a year.
Seven years ago, an even faster and
cheaper American manufacturing technology was used to
make this little carbon fiber test part, which doubles as a carbon cap.
In one minute, and you can tell from the sound how immensely stiff and
strong it is.
Don't worry about dropping it.
It's tougher than titanium.

without even scuffing it.
But such manufacturing techniques can scale to automotive speed and
cost with aerospace performance.
They can save four-fifths of the capital needed to make autos.

crash energy per pound of steel.

the Detroit formation, a very perspective play.

thirds and combined with triple to quintupled efficiency airplanes now on
